evidence: Docstring asks whether there exists a maximal Sidon set A ⊂ {1,...,N} of size O(N^{1/3}); the problem is about Sidon sets.
Docstring: the greedy construction of a maximal Sidon set in {1,...,N} has size ≫ N^{1/3}.
Docstring: Ruzsa [Ru98b] constructed a maximal Sidon set of size ≪ (N log N)^{1/3}.
The B₂[g] sets of the statement generalize Sidon sets (B₂[1] iff Sidon per lemma b2_one); erdosproblems.com tags the problem 'sidon sets'.
Docstring: for an infinite Sidon set A, liminf |A ∩ {1,...,N}| N^{-1/2} (log N)^{1/2} < ∞ [ESS94].
Docstring: liminf |A ∩ {1,...,N}| N^{-1/2} = 0 for any infinite Sidon set A.
Docstring asks whether the set of odd integers not of the form 2^k+p is the union of an infinite arithmetic progression and a set of density 0.
The statement concerns odd integers of the form 2^k + p with p prime; erdosproblems.com tags the problem 'primes'.
Docstring: 'Romanoff [Ro34] showed that the set of odd integers of this form has positive density' — the decl states Romanov's 1934 theorem that numbers 2^k+p have positive lower density.
Docstring: 'Using covering congruences Erdős [Er50] proved that the set of odd integers which are not of this form contains an infinite arithmetic progression.'
The decl asserts an infinite arithmetic progression {a + m*d} inside Erdos16Set.
h(n) is the least number of colours so every four-term arithmetic progression in {1,...,n} contains at least three distinct colours; this decl bounds h(n) ≪ n^{2/3}.
Asks for a better upper bound on h(n), the colouring number forcing three colours in every four-term arithmetic progression.
Asks for a better lower bound on h(n), the colouring number forcing three colours in every four-term arithmetic progression.
Docstring: Hunter's observation plus Kelley-Meka bounds imply h(N) ≫ exp(c(log N)^{1/12}) for the four-term-AP colouring function h.
F(N) counts the largest subset of {1,...,N} with no 3-term arithmetic progression of the form n, 2n, 3n (the NonTernary condition); this asks for lim F(N)/N.
Asks whether lim F(N)/N is irrational, where F counts sets avoiding progressions n, 2n, 3n.
Docstring: the limit F(N)/N exists (Graham, Spencer, and Witsenhausen), for F avoiding progressions n, 2n, 3n.
Docstring: 'Erdős Problem 17. Are there infinitely many cluster primes?'
Docstring: Blecksmith, Erdős, and Selfridge [BES99] proved the upper bound π^C(x) ≪_A x(log x)^{-A} for the cluster-prime counting function.
Docstring: Elsholtz [El03] refined the cluster-prime counting bound to π^C(x) ≪ x exp(-c(log log x)^2).
erdosproblems.com/170 is 'sparse ruler problem'; the decl asks for lim F(N)/√N where F(N) is the minimum number of marks of an N-perfect ruler.
Docstring defines an N-perfect ruler (difference basis) as a mark set measuring every k ≤ N; the problem minimizes its cardinality.
Docstring: the limit exists (Erdős-Gál) with Leech's lower bound and Wichmann's ruler upper bound √3 for the sparse-ruler quantity F(N)/√N.
Asks whether any finite colouring of ℕ admits arbitrarily large finite A with all sums and products of distinct elements monochromatic; erdosproblems.com tags it 'ramsey theory'.

The statement asks for a ±1 function balancing partial sums over infinitely many integer sequences (Beck's 'discrepancy problem: balancing infinite dimensional vectors'); erdosproblems.com tags it 'discrepancy'.
